summary: Jeff Atwood analyzes the 2010 Gawker network breach to extract lessons about password security for web developers. Gawker made critical mistakes: storing passwords rather than salted hashes, using archaic DES encryption that truncated passwords to 8 characters, and requiring users to create site-specific credentials. Users who authenticated via Facebook Connect or Twitter were unaffected. Atwood argues this breach exemplifies the fundamental problem with website passwords—the more sites you trust with credentials, the more likely one will be compromised. He advocates for federated authentication through trusted third parties like Google, Facebook, or OpenID.
key_points: Never store user passwords directly—always store salted hashes, yet this basic security practice is routinely ignored
The more websites you create unique logins for, the higher your statistical probability of being compromised by at least one incompetent site
Users who authenticated via third-party services (Facebook Connect, Twitter) were completely unaffected by the breach
Most users choose weak passwords and reuse them across sites, creating cascading vulnerabilities when any single site is breached
Federated authentication through trusted providers centralizes risk to companies with actual security competence
money_quotes: You should never, ever store user passwords. If you do, you're storing passwords incorrectly.
Here's the dirty truth about website passwords: the internet is full of websites exactly like the Gawker network.

The more web sites you visit, the more networks you touch and trust with a username and password combination – the greater the odds that at least one of those networks will be compromised exactly like Gawker was.
Does the need to post a comment on Gizmodo really justify polluting the world with yet another username and password?
I trust Google a heck of a lot more than I trust J. Random Website.
We're all better off without them.
When your corporate mission is to succeed by exploiting every quasi-legal trick in the book, surely you can't be surprised when you get the same treatment in return.
key_insight: The fundamental problem with web passwords isn't just that sites implement them poorly—it's that the proliferation of site-specific credentials mathematically guarantees eventual compromise, making federated authentication through trusted providers the only sensible solution.
